name: Clocks — Angles Between the Hands
whatIsIt: A clock face is a circle of 360°, split into 12 hours, so each hour mark is 30° apart. The MINUTE hand sweeps a full 360° in 60 minutes → 6° per minute. The HOUR hand sweeps 360° in 12 hours → only 0.5° per minute (it creeps as the minutes pass). The angle between the hands at H hours M minutes is |30H − 5.5M| degrees (take the smaller of that and 360 minus it).
whyNeeded: Clock-angle puzzles are a favourite in SSC, Bank, Railway and campus aptitude tests. They also sharpen the powerful idea of two things moving at different speeds around a circle — the same maths as gears, laps and orbits.
realLifeUses: Puzzle questions: 'angle between the hands at 3:20?'
When do the hands overlap or make a straight line?
Understanding why the hour hand isn't exactly on the hour mark
Gears and dials that turn at different rates
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: 360° ÷ 12 = 30°, the gap between two hour numbers.
0.5° per minute — it drifts forward as minutes pass.
Angle = |30H − 5.5M|; if over 180°, use 360 − that for the smaller angle.

story.title: The Angle at Half Past Three
story.text: Sameer asks, 'What's the angle between the hands at 3:30?' His aunt uses the rule: 'Hour part: 30 × 3 = 90. Minute part: 5.5 × 30 = 165. The angle is |90 − 165| = 75°.' Sameer is surprised it isn't 90° — 'because the hour hand has already crept halfway to 4 by 3:30,' she explains. 'At exactly 3:00 it would be 30 × 3 − 0 = 90°, but those extra 30 minutes moved the hour hand 15° forward.'
story.extractedProblem: Find the angle between the clock hands at 3:30.

visual.caption: Each hour mark is 30°. Minute hand 6°/min, hour hand only 0.5°/min. Angle = |30H − 5.5M|; the hour hand's creep is why 3:30 is 75°, not 90°.

teachingGallery.title: Angle = |30H − 5.5M|
teachingGallery.note: Hour marks are 30° apart. The hour hand drifts 0.5° each minute, so use 5.5M not 6M.

standardMethod.summary: Use angle = |30H − 5.5M|; if it exceeds 180°, subtract from 360 for the smaller angle.
steps: Hour part = 30 × H (degrees for the hour).
Minute part = 5.5 × M (0.5 for the drifting hour hand + 5.5 handles both).
If the result is more than 180°, the smaller angle is 360 − result.

alternateMethods.name: Speed-gap method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To see WHY the formula works.
steps: They separate at 6 − 0.5 = 5.5° per minute from the top of the hour.
Combine with the 30° per hour head start of the hour hand.
alternateMethods.example: At 3:30 the minute hand is at 180°, hour hand at 90 + 15 = 105° → gap 75°.

workedExamples.problem: Find the angle between the hands at 3:00.
Find the angle between the hands at 2:20.
steps: 50° is under 180°, so that's the answer.

trickPractice.intro: Plug hour and minute into the formula; if over 180°, subtract from 360.

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming the hour hand stays exactly on the hour mark as minutes pass (getting 90° at 3:30).
commonMistakes.fix: The hour hand CREEPS — 0.5° every minute. By 3:30 it has moved 15° past the 3. That's why the formula uses 5.5M, and 3:30 is 75°, not 90°.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reporting the big (reflex) angle when the smaller angle is expected.
commonMistakes.fix: If |30H − 5.5M| comes out above 180°, subtract it from 360 to get the usual (smaller) angle between the hands.

easy.q: How far does the minute hand turn in one minute?

challenge.q: At 3:30 the angle is 75°, not 90°, because…

options.label: The hour hand has crept past the 3

challenge.answer: The hour hand has crept past the 3

questions.q: The hour hand moves how many degrees per minute?

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend the clock-angle formula |30H − 5.5M|. Use 3:30 to show why the answer is 75° and not 90° — because the hour hand creeps 0.5° every minute and has moved 15° past the 3.

revisionCard.summary: Hour marks 30° apart. Minute hand 6°/min, hour hand 0.5°/min. Angle = |30H − 5.5M|; if over 180°, use 360 − it. The hour hand's creep (5.5M, not 6M) is the whole trick — 3:30 gives 75°, not 90°.

gameMission.title: Beat the Clock Angles
gameMission.brief: Snap the angle between the hands for any time! Use the 30H − 5.5M rule, keep the smaller angle, and never freeze the creeping hour hand.

realLifeProject: Pick five random times on a real clock. Predict each angle with |30H − 5.5M|, then hold a protractor to the clock face (or estimate) and check how close you were.

rememberIt.unpack: 30° per hour mark. Hour hand creeps 0.5°/min. Over 180° → take 360 − it.

formulas.name: Hand angle
formulas.whenToUse: Angle between the hands at H:M.

studentQuestions.a: The minute hand gains 6° a minute but the hour hand also creeps 0.5° a minute, so their relative movement is 6 − 0.5 = 5.5° per minute.

studentQuestions.q: How many times do the hands overlap in 12 hours?
studentQuestions.a: 11 times — not 12 — because the hour hand keeps moving, so it takes a little over an hour for the minute hand to catch up each time.
